Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-03-191
Dear Mr. Berger:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of City of Santa
Paula Mayor John Procter, Vice Mayor Gabino Aguirre, and Councilmembers Rick
Cook, Mary-Ann Krause and Ray Lu-na regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act').r This letter should not be construed as advice on
any decisions of the Santa Paula City Council that may have already taken place. Our
advice is based on the facts presented in your request; the Fair Political Practices
Commission ("Commission') does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (In
re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)
QUESTTONS
l. Do Mayor Procter, Vice Mayor Aguirre, or Councilmernbers Cook, Krause or
Luna, have conflicts of interest prohibiting their making, participating in making, or
influencing decisions concerning a proposed amendment to the city's general plan, a pre-
annexation agreement, and a reimbursement agreement, (collectively, "decisions')
pertaining to the proposed development of Fagan Canyon?
2. If one or more of these officials has a conflict of interest, would the "public
generally'' or'legally required participation"2 exceptions permit his or her involvement
in these decisions?
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Cornrnission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18-997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Although your request questioned applicability of the "rule of necessity," a common law
doctrine, we assume that you are inquiring about the "legally required participation" exception, which is an
exception under the Act that is generally similar to this common law rule.


File No. 4-03-191
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. Conflict of Interest
Mayor Procter: Mayor Procter's principal residence is located within 500 feet of
the proposed boundaries of the Fagan Canyon Project. It is presumed under the
Commission's regulations, therefore, that these decisions will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect upon his real property. For this reason he has a
conflict of interest concerning these decisions.3
Vice Mayor Aeruirre: Vice Mayor Aguirre's principal residence is located within
500 feet of the proposed boundaries of the Fagan Canyon Project. It is presumed under
the Commission's regulations, therefore, that these decisions will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect upon his real property. For this reason he has a
conflict of interest concerning these decisions.
Councilmernber Cook: Councilmember Cook's principal residence is located
more than 500 feet from the proposed boundaries of the Fagan Canyon Project. It is
presumed under the Commission's regulations, therefore, that these decisions will not
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the council member's
economic interest in his principal residence. As long as this presumption is not rebutted,
he will not have a conflict of interest concerning these decisions, based on his principal
residence.
However, the purchaser of Councilmember Cook's former residence is a source of
income to him, and for that reason also numbers among his economic interests. Since
this purchaser is not the initiator, a named party in, or otherwise the subject of, the
proceedings in which these decisions will be made, Councilmernber Cook will have a
conflict of interest on this basis only if these decisions will affect the purchaser's assets
or liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000 or more, or affects the purchaser's
interest in real property in a manner considered material under regulation 18705.2(b).4
Councilmember Krause: Councilmember Krause's principal residence is located
more than 500 feet from the proposed boundaries of the Fagan Canyon Project. It is
presumed under the Commission's regulations that these decisions will not have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the council member's economic
interest in her principal residence. As long as this presumption is not rebutted, she will
not have a conflict of interest disqualifying her from participating in these decisions.
3 In addition, Mayor Procter's l/86 interest in the kust which owns his principal residence may
result in his having an economic interest in any other real property owned by the trust and in sources of
income to the trust. Ifsuch is the case, the project's reasonably foreseeable financial effects upon these
interests, if deemed material, maypresent a further conflict of interest. This is a fact-dependent analysis
and your information contains no facts allowing us to make this determination.
a Regulation 18705.2(b) provides the materiality standard applicable to a public official's
economic interest in real property that is indirectly involved in the governmental decisions.


File No. A-03-191
Page No. 3
Councilmember Luna: Councilmernber Luna's principal residence is located more
than 500 feet from the proposed boundaries of the Fagan Canyon Project. It is presumed
under the Commission's regulations that these decisions will not have areasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on the council member's economic interest in his
principal residence. As long as this presumption is not rebutted, he will not have a
conflict of interest disqualifuing him from participating in these decisions.
2. Exceptions
Public Generally: Although the information you provide indicates that these
decisions will affect a significant segment of the public generally, this information is not
suffrcient to show that Mayor Procter and Vice Mayor Aguirre will be affected in a
manner that is substantially similar to the effect upon this significant segment of the
public. Critical to this showing would be an assessment of the financial consequences to
affected homeowners of the increased traffic that will result from the Fagan Canyon
Project. There may be other effects that should also be considered (such as effects on
property assessments and millage rates, and changes in real estate valuations).
Ultimately, this is a question of fact for the public officials to decide and not the
Commission.
Legally Required Participation: Since a quonrm of the city council does not have
a disqualifting conflict of interest and mayparticipate in these decisions, the
participation of Mayor Procter and Vice Mayor Aguirre is not legally required. This
presumes that under local ordinance or other applicable law these decisions may be made
by a three-member vote of the city council and that the presumption applicable to these
three members' economic interests in their principal residences is not rebuffed under their
funher evaluation of the facts.
FACTS
The City of Santa Paula is a general law city with a city manager form of
governmant, including a five-member city council and a seven-member planning
commission. Santa Paula's jurisdictional boundaries encompass approximately 4.4
square miles (an area of approximately 2,908 acres), subdivided into 6,833 parcels of real
estateownedbyatotal of 6,142propertyowners. There are3,643 owner-occupied
residential units located in the city. Santa Paula has a population of 28,598 individuals,
averagSng 3.49 individuals per household.
Highway 126, which bisects the city proximate to its southern boundary, is the
only regional transportation route into or out of Santa Paula. Santa Paula's commercial
center is roughly parallel to, and several blocks north of, Highway 126. Access by city
residents to Santa Paula's commercial center and to Highway 126 is gained through
seven major traffic corridors: Ojai Rd./Highway 150, 1Otr' St.Alighway 150, Palm Ave.,
Peck Rd., Santa Paula St., Main St., and Harvard Blvd./Telegraph Rd.


File No. 4-03-191
Page No. 4
A developer is proposing to develop an area of approximately 2,000 acres situated
immediately adjacent to Santa Paula's northern boundary (the'?agan Canyon Project" or
"Project"). Fagan Canyon is currently undeveloped, mountainous terrain. Due to this
topography, development within the overall project areacan only occur within a limited
area, generally adjacent to Santa Paula's existing boundaries. City records show that
there are 416 parcels of city real estate located within 500 feet of the boundaries of this
project. These records also show that 283 property owners (approximat ely 4.6Yo of the
city's total number of property owners) reside within 500 feet of the proposed boundaries
of the Fagan Canyon Project. This project consists of between 1,350 and2,500
residential dwelling units, parks, recreational areas, and other public and private
improvements. The developerproposes that the city annex Fagan Canyon, which would
increase the city's area by approximately 75%o and, if all 2,500 residential dwelling units
are built, increase the city's population by up to 10,000 individuals (a35% population
increase).
The city's commuting traffrc and traffic from outlaying areas to the city's center
presently travels through one or more of seven existing traffic corridors. The Fagan
Canyon Project would not lead to a re-location of any of these corridors. Instead,
although the topography limits ingress to and egress from Fagan Canyon to four access
points, the traffic generated by the Project's residents will flow through one or more of
the city's existing seven traffic corridors. It is expected that the increased traffic flow
through some of these corridors will be substantial (as much as30o/o on one corridor and
52Yo on another). City fraffic engineers have projected traffic flows from the Project and
regardless of whether 1,350 or 2,500 homes are built, conclude that it is probable that all
of the city's residents will be affected in their vehicular travel, regardless of their distance
from the Project boundaries.
According to the city's studies, 234propefiy owners live within 500 feet of both
the Project boundaries and one of these traffic corridors. An additional 1,690 property
owners live within 500 feet of these traffic corridors. Based upon these numbers, 27.5%
of the city's property owners live within 500 feet of these seven kaffic corridors and
32.1% live within 500 feet of one of the traffic corridors or the Project boundaries.
Councilmember Krause and Luna each live within 500 feet of one of these traffic
corridors. Each of the council members, including Mayor Procter and Vice Mayor
Aguirre, would need to use at least one of the traffic corridors in order to travel from
his/her principal residence to other parts of the city, and/or to enter or leave the city
through Highway 126.
Numerous governmental approvals will be required in order to develop the Fagan
Canyon Project. The developer will need to obtain from the city: an amendment to the
city's general plan, approval of a pre-annexation agreernent, approval of a specific plan,
certification of a final environmental impact report, zoning changes, approval of a
development agreement, and approval of a reimbursement agreernent. In addition, a
petition for annexation and application to the Local Area Formation Commission to
annex Fagan Canyon will have to be approved. The city must also approve cancellation
of Williamson Act agricultural contracts and grant tentative map approvals under the


File No. A-03-191
Page No. 5
Subdivisions Map Act. Both the city planning commission and city council must
consider these matters.
At present, the developer has only filed for city approval of a general plan
amendment, the pre-annexation agreement, and the reimbursement agreement. These
two agreements, if approved by the city, would establish procedures for furtherplanning
of the Project and create a mechanism to reimburse the city for its related planning costs.
The general plan amendment is to increase the number of homes potentially permitted in
development of Fagan Canyon from the presently authorized number of 455 to the
developer's planned number, which is 2,500.
Mayor Procter's principal residence is residential real property owned by an
irrevocable living trust, in which he has a 1/86 interest. He pays no rent in connection
with his occupancy of this property, although the fair market rental value may be close to
$2,000 per month. Vice Mayor Aguirre's owns his principal residence, which has a fair
market value that exceeds $2,000. This property is located within 500 feet of the
boundaries of the Fagan Canyon Project. Recently, Councilmember Cook purchased
residential real property which is now his principal residence. This property has a fair
market value that is more than $2,000 and is located more than 500 feet from the Project
boundaries. His prior residence was located within 500 feet of the Project boundaries.
This prior residence was recently sold and Councilmember Cook's proceeds from that
sale exceeded $500. Councilmembers Krause and Luna each own homes that are located
more than 500 feet from the Project boundaries. Each of their homes is valued in excess
of $2,000.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step
standard analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualifuing conflict of interest
(regulation 18700, subdivisions (bxl) - (8)), which is discussed below. The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest may exist whenever a public official makes a
govefirmental decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
one or more of his or her financial interests.
l. & 2. Are the named individuals public officials who will make, participate in
making or influence a goyernmental decision?
The conflict-of-interest prohibitions only apply to public officials. As you
correctly note in your request, the named individuals are public officials (section 82048;
regulation 18701(a)) and as a city council member,s unless disqualified under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act, each will make, participate in making, and
influence govemmental decisions, including decisions that concern amending the city's
5 Although not explicitly stated, the information you provide suggests that Santa Paula's mayor
and vice mayor are also voting members of the city council.


FileNo. 4-03-191
Page No. 6
general plan, and the two proposed agreements submitted by the Fagan Canyon Project's
developer.6
3. What are these officials'economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from economic interests. These economic interests are described at regulations 18703.1
through 18703.5, inclusive. Under the facts you provide, the specific economic interestsT
applicable to these public officials are as follows:
Real Property -- Apublic official has an economic interest in real property in
which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b);
regulation 18703.2.) An indirect investment or interest in real property means, among
other things, any real property owned by any business entity or trust in which the official
owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially a l0-percent interest or greater. (Section 82033.)
An interest in real property includes leaseholds (other than a periodic tenancy of one
month or less), when the property is both located within the jurisdiction and has a fair
market value of $2,000 or more. (Section 82033; regulation 18233.)
Sources of Income -- A public official has an economic interest in any source of
income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months
prior to the decision at issue. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3.) Income, for this
pu{pose, includes apro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which
the individual (or his or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-
percent or greater interest. (Section 82030(a).)
Mayor Procter: Mayor Procter does not own the property which comprises his
principal residence. Instead, he occupies the property rent-free as a 1/86 interest holder
in the trust which owns this property.s The fair market value of his right to occupy the
premises, you indicate, may be close to $2,000 per month. Although he does not pay
6 If a public official's office is listed in section 87200 (*87200 filers" include city council
members and members ofplanning commissions) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision
noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) inunediately prior to the discussion of the item,
verbally identi$ each tlpe of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details ofthe economic
interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.5(bXlXB), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or
herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and./or vote on the item. For closed
sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member ofthe public regarding personal interests,
special rules found in regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105). Since these
officials are either members of the city planning commission or city council (which are positions
enumerated in section 87200), these requirements apply to them.
7 In addition to the economic interests separately listed in section 87103, a public official always
has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, and may have a conflict of interest in any decision
foreseeably resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities ofthe
official or his or her immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a l2-month period.
(Regulations I 8703.5 and I 8705.5.)
E Presumably, his rent-free occupancy of this properly arises as a form of dishibution to Mayor
Procter as a beneficiary under the tust.


File No. A-03-191
Page No. 7
rent, since he has occupied this property for more than one month, the fair market value
of leasehold over the period of his occupancy equals, or is greater than, $2,000. His
leasehold interest in this property is an economic interest to him.
Under certain circumstances the property, income, and investments of a trust may
also be considered among a public official's economic interests. Regulation 18234
describes these circumstances as follows:
"(a) An official has an economic interest in the pro rata share of the
interests in real property, sources of income, and investments of a
trust in which the official has a direct, indirect, or beneficial interest
of 10 percent or greater."
However, even when the official's interest in a trust is 10 percent or greater,
regulation 18234limits the circumstances under which the beneficiary of a trust will be
considered to have an economic interest in his pro rata share of the trust's real property,
investments and in sources of income to the trust. (Sullivan Advice Letter, No. A-95-
234.) The relevant potential limitation under the facts you provide is found in
subdivision lSTa@)Q) -- the official is:
*(2) Abeneficiary and:
(A) Presently receives income; or
(B) Has an irrevocable future right to receive income or principal. For
purposes of this subsection, an individual has an irrevocable future right
to receive income or principal if the trust is irrevocable, unless one of
the following applies:
(i) Powers exist to consume, invade, or appoint the principal for the
benefit of beneficiaries other than the official and such powers are not
limited by an ascertainable standard relating to the health, education,
support, or maintenance of the beneficiaries; or
(ii) Under the terms of the trust, someone other than the official can
designate the persons who shall possess or enjoy the trust property or
income."
Under the facts you provide, Mayor Procter is a greater than 10 percent (1/8m)
beneficiary under the trust and, since the trust is irrevocable, apparently has an
irrevocable future right to receive trust income or principal. Moreover, he is presently
receiving income from the trust in the form of rent-free occupancy of trust real estate.
Thus, Mayor Procter has an economic interest in the real property which constitutes his
principal residence, on this basis as well.e
e Mayor Procter should be aware that since he has a greater than l0%o interest in this trust, he also
has a pro rata interest in the income and real property of the trust. Thus, he might have otler sources of
income or real property that are economic interests to him. The facts you supply are silent in this regard.
Thus, we are unable to offer you any advice as to whetler Mayor Procter has additional economic interests
as a result of his 1/86 interest in this trust. Nevertheless, he should be aware of this potential and take it
into account when determining whether he has a disqualifuing conflict of interest.

Vice Mayor Aguirre: Vice Mayor Aguirre owns his principal residence, the fair
market value of which is $2,000 or more. Accordingly, his principal residence is an
economic interest to him.
Councilmember Cook: Councilmernber Cook owns his principal residence, the
fair market value of which is $2,000 or more. Accordingly, his principal residence is an
economic interbst to him. In addition, the purchaser of his former residence has provided
him with income of $500 or more within the past several months, as a result of that sale.
The purchaser is thus a source of income to him and numbers among his economic
interests.lo
Councilmember Krause: Councilmember Krause owns her principal residence,
the fair market value of which is $2,000 or more. Accordingly, her principal residence is
an economic interest to her.
Councilmember Luna: Councilmember Luna owns his principal residence, the fair
market value of which is $2,000 or more. Accordingly, his principal residence is an
economic interest to him.
4. Are these economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
Source of Income -- A person, including a source of income, in which a public
o{ficial has an economic interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if that
person, either directly or by an agent initiates a proceeding by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request, or is a named party in, or is the subject of a proceeding
before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18704.1(a)(1)-(a)(2).) A source
of income is the subject of a proceeding concerning a decision before the official or the
official's agency if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the source of
income. (Regulation 187 04.1 (a)Q).)
Real Property - Under your facts, the appropriate standard for determining direct
or indirect involvement of an official's economic interest in real property is found in
regulation I 87 0a.2@)(l):
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of that real
property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the governmental decision. . . ." In this instance, the
single amendment proposed to the city's general plan is to increase the allowable
residential units forpotential development in Fagan Canyon from 455 units to 2,500
units. Although property outside the proposed boundaries of the Fagan Canyon Project is
expected to be affected by the Project (e.g., enhanced property values throughout the city
and additional traffic on the city's existing traffic corridors), these effects do not require
l0 This purchaser will be corsidered to be a source of income to, and economic interest of,
Councilmember Cook's only until the end of the l2-month period immediately following the date of the
purchase of this real property. (Regulation 18703.3(a).)

the city council to make decisions about property outside these boundaries while they
consider the proposed requests. For instance, although kaffic volume will be affected on
the city's traffic corridors, none of these corridors will be physically relocated and the
city is not considering taking additional property at this time to expand the present size of
these traffic corridors. Thus, the property which is the subject of this decision is the
Fagan Canyon development area."
Mayor Procter: Mayor Procter's principal residence is located within 500 feet
from the proposed boundaries of the Fagan Canyon Project. Thus, this economic interest
will be directly involved in these decisions.
Vice Mayor Aguirre: Vice Mayor Aguirre's principal residence is located within
500 feet from the proposed boundaries of the Fagan Canyon Project. Thus, this economic
interest will be directly involved in these decisions.
Councilmernber Cook: Councilmember Cook's principal residence is located
more than 500 feet from the proposed boundaries of the Fagan Canyon Project. Thus,
this economic interest will be indirectly involved in these decisions.
In his request for advice dated fuly 2l,2OO3,tz Councilmernber Cook identified
his adult son as the purchaser of his former residence. Presumably, the purchaser did not,
either directly or by an agent, initiate the proceeding before the city council by filing the
request for an amendment of the city's general plan. Therefore, he is not a named party
with respect to that request and is not the subject of that proceeding. Thus, the purchaser
will be indirectly involved in these decisions.
Councilmember Krarise: Councilmernber Krause's principal residence is located
more than 500 feet from the proposed boundaries of the Fagan Canyon Project. Thus,
this economic interest will be indirectly involved in these decisions.
Councilmember Luna: Councilmember Luna's principal residence is located more
than 500 feet from the proposed boundaries of the Fagan Canyon Project. Thus, this
economic interest will be indirectly involved in these decisions.
ll This description of "the real property which is the subject of the governmental decision" is
uniquely addressed to the matters upon which you have sought advice. Although you describe other, future
decisions that will have to be made by the city's planning commission and the city council, including
decisions to further amend the city's general plan, our present advice concerning the real property that is
the subject of the city counsel's decisions may or may not apply in context of these future general plan
decisions.
" Since the written advice we are providing in response to your request fully addresses the
matters raised in Councilmember Cook's July 21, 2003, request for advice (Advice Request W-03-161), we
decline to provide separate advice to Councilmember Cook in response to this prior request. (Regulation
18329(bX8XF), copy enclosed.) By separate letter we are concurrently withdrawing his individual request.

5-6. What are the applicable materiality standards and is it reasonably foreseeable
that the financial effects of the governmental decisions upon these officialst
economic interests wiII meet this materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
agency's decision. We discuss below only the materiality standards that correspond to
the tlpe of involvernent identified in step 4, above.
Individuals as Sources of Income - Councilmember Cook
When a source of income to a public o{ficial is an individual who is indirectly
involved in the governmental decision, the applicable materiality standard is found at
regulation I 8705.3(b)(3):
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $1,000 or more; or
(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under. . . [regulation] I 8705.2(b)."
Note that the analysis in regulation 18705.2(b) is to be applied to real property
owned or leased by an individual who is a source of income to a public official. Under
regulation 18705.2b), a decision is presumed not to have a material financial effect on
real property, when that property is indirectly involved in a govemmental decision. This
presumption may be rebutted by proof that specific circumstances exist regarding the
financial effect of a proposed decision and the nature of the real property which make it
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on that real
property.
Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered are found in
regulation 18705.2@)(2XA) - (C):
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels,
air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."
As indicated above, these circumstances include substantial effects on traffic, such as the
effects on the traffic corridors described in the information you provide. It is possible


File No. A-03-l9l
Page No. 11
that these efflects on traffic will have a financial effect on the purchaser's interest in his
real property, deemed material under regulation 18705.2@). If so, the presumption
would be rebutted and Councilmember Cook would have a conflict of interest on this
basis. This is a question of fact and is thus a matter for Councilmember Cook, and not
the Commission, to determine.13
Real Property - All Officials
When real property that is an economic interest of a public official is directly
involved in a governmental decision, there is a presumption under the Commission's
regulations that the financial effect of the decision upon the real property is material.
(Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) and (a)Q).) This presumption can be rebutted by proof that it
is not reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have any financial effect at all on the
real property. (Regulati on 187 05.2(a)(1 ).)
Under the Commission's regulations, it is presumed that governmental decisions
will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon indirectly involved
real property interests. (Regulations 18705.2(bxl) and (b)(2).) This presumption may be
rebutted by utilizing specific circumstances described in the Commission's regulations or
other proof. (Regulations I 8705.2(bX I XA)-(C), and 187 05.2@)(2XA)-(E).)
Mayor Procter: Mayor Procter's economic interest in his principal residence is
directly involved in decisions concerning the proposed general plan amendment. It is
presumed under regulation 18705.2(a)(1) that his economic interest in his real property
will be materially financially affected by these decisions.
Vice Mayor Aeuirre: Vice Mayor Aguirre's economic interest in his principal
residence is directly involved in decisions concerning the proposed general plan
amendment. It is presumed under regulation 18705.2(a)(l) that his economic interest in
his real property will be materially financially affected by these decisions.
Councilmember Cook: Councilmernber Cook's economic interest in his principal
residence is indirectly involved in decisions concerning the proposed general plan
amendment. It is presumed under the Commission's regulations that these decisions will
not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon Councilmember Cook's
interests in his principal residence. (Regulations 18705.2(b)(1).)
13 In our prior advice to Councilmember Cook (Cook Adice Letter, No. A-93-114), we noted his
representation that the majority of the new trafiic flow in and out of Fagan Canyon would be within 500
feet of from what is now his former residence. Assuming that this increased trafflc flow would have a
financial effect upon the value of his former residence, we concluded in our prior advice that this financial
effect would be material under the Commission's regulations. In the additional information which you
supply, you describe that taffic effects in the traffic corridors increase as the distance from Fagan Canyon
increases. Thus, there is a factual question whether the effects of this Project on the city's traffic corridors
will rebut the presumption that the Project will have no material financial effect on the purchaser's real
property. (See our discussion which follows.)


File No. 4-03-191
Page No. 12
However, there are specific circumstances in the present case which may rebut
this presumption. According to the report of the city's traffic engineer, the Project will
substantially affect the traffic flow along the city's seven traffic corridors (by as much as
52Yo alongone corridor). According to your information, Councilmember Cook's
current principal residence is not located within one of these traffic corridors and it
appears that the anticipated increased traffic flow will not materially impact the
neighborhood in which his residence is located. Therefore, at this point the presumption
is not rebutted. However, this is a factual question which the council member must
decide.
Council Mernbers Krause and Luna: Our analysis above with respect to
Councilmernber Cook's economic interest in his principal residence is equally applicable
to Councilmembers Krause and Luna's economic interests in their respective principal
residences. As noted above, although decisions concerning the general plan amendments
presumably will not have a material financial effect upon these economic interests, it is
possible that the Project's impact upon traffic flow may have a financial effect on their
residences that will rebut this presumption. This is especially a concern with respect to
Councilmember Luna, who resides within a corridor that the city's traffic engineer
anticipates will experience increased traffic flow with the development of Fagan Canyon.

sections.facts:
The City of Santa Paula is a general law city with a city manager form of
governmant, including a five-member city council and a seven-member planning
commission. Santa Paula's jurisdictional boundaries encompass approximately 4.4
square miles (an area of approximately 2,908 acres), subdivided into 6,833 parcels of real
estateownedbyatotal of 6,142propertyowners. There are3,643 owner-occupied
residential units located in the city. Santa Paula has a population of 28,598 individuals,
averagSng 3.49 individuals per household.
Highway 126, which bisects the city proximate to its southern boundary, is the
only regional transportation route into or out of Santa Paula. Santa Paula's commercial
center is roughly parallel to, and several blocks north of, Highway 126. Access by city
residents to Santa Paula's commercial center and to Highway 126 is gained through
seven major traffic corridors: Ojai Rd./Highway 150, 1Otr' St.Alighway 150, Palm Ave.,
Peck Rd., Santa Paula St., Main St., and Harvard Blvd./Telegraph Rd.
A developer is proposing to develop an area of approximately 2,000 acres situated
immediately adjacent to Santa Paula's northern boundary (the'?agan Canyon Project" or
"Project"). Fagan Canyon is currently undeveloped, mountainous terrain. Due to this
topography, development within the overall project areacan only occur within a limited
area, generally adjacent to Santa Paula's existing boundaries. City records show that
there are 416 parcels of city real estate located within 500 feet of the boundaries of this
project. These records also show that 283 property owners (approximat ely 4.6Yo of the
city's total number of property owners) reside within 500 feet of the proposed boundaries
of the Fagan Canyon Project. This project consists of between 1,350 and2,500
residential dwelling units, parks, recreational areas, and other public and private
improvements. The developerproposes that the city annex Fagan Canyon, which would
increase the city's area by approximately 75%o and, if all 2,500 residential dwelling units
are built, increase the city's population by up to 10,000 individuals (a35% population
increase).
The city's commuting traffrc and traffic from outlaying areas to the city's center
presently travels through one or more of seven existing traffic corridors. The Fagan
Canyon Project would not lead to a re-location of any of these corridors. Instead,
although the topography limits ingress to and egress from Fagan Canyon to four access
points, the traffic generated by the Project's residents will flow through one or more of
the city's existing seven traffic corridors. It is expected that the increased traffic flow
through some of these corridors will be substantial (as much as30o/o on one corridor and
52Yo on another). City fraffic engineers have projected traffic flows from the Project and
regardless of whether 1,350 or 2,500 homes are built, conclude that it is probable that all
of the city's residents will be affected in their vehicular travel, regardless of their distance
from the Project boundaries.
According to the city's studies, 234propefiy owners live within 500 feet of both
the Project boundaries and one of these traffic corridors. An additional 1,690 property
owners live within 500 feet of these traffic corridors. Based upon these numbers, 27.5%
of the city's property owners live within 500 feet of these seven kaffic corridors and
32.1% live within 500 feet of one of the traffic corridors or the Project boundaries.
Councilmember Krause and Luna each live within 500 feet of one of these traffic
corridors. Each of the council members, including Mayor Procter and Vice Mayor
Aguirre, would need to use at least one of the traffic corridors in order to travel from
his/her principal residence to other parts of the city, and/or to enter or leave the city
through Highway 126.
Numerous governmental approvals will be required in order to develop the Fagan
Canyon Project. The developer will need to obtain from the city: an amendment to the
city's general plan, approval of a pre-annexation agreernent, approval of a specific plan,
certification of a final environmental impact report, zoning changes, approval of a
development agreement, and approval of a reimbursement agreernent. In addition, a
petition for annexation and application to the Local Area Formation Commission to
annex Fagan Canyon will have to be approved. The city must also approve cancellation
of Williamson Act agricultural contracts and grant tentative map approvals under the
Subdivisions Map Act. Both the city planning commission and city council must
consider these matters.
At present, the developer has only filed for city approval of a general plan
amendment, the pre-annexation agreement, and the reimbursement agreement. These
two agreements, if approved by the city, would establish procedures for furtherplanning
of the Project and create a mechanism to reimburse the city for its related planning costs.
The general plan amendment is to increase the number of homes potentially permitted in
development of Fagan Canyon from the presently authorized number of 455 to the
developer's planned number, which is 2,500.
Mayor Procter's principal residence is residential real property owned by an
irrevocable living trust, in which he has a 1/86 interest. He pays no rent in connection
with his occupancy of this property, although the fair market rental value may be close to
$2,000 per month. Vice Mayor Aguirre's owns his principal residence, which has a fair
market value that exceeds $2,000. This property is located within 500 feet of the
boundaries of the Fagan Canyon Project. Recently, Councilmember Cook purchased
residential real property which is now his principal residence. This property has a fair
market value that is more than $2,000 and is located more than 500 feet from the Project
boundaries. His prior residence was located within 500 feet of the Project boundaries.
This prior residence was recently sold and Councilmember Cook's proceeds from that
sale exceeded $500. Councilmembers Krause and Luna each own homes that are located
more than 500 feet from the Project boundaries. Each of their homes is valued in excess
of $2,000.

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step
standard analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualifuing conflict of interest
(regulation 18700, subdivisions (bxl) - (8)), which is discussed below. The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest may exist whenever a public official makes a
govefirmental decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
one or more of his or her financial interests.

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
agency's decision. We discuss below only the materiality standards that correspond to
the tlpe of involvernent identified in step 4, above.

When a source of income to a public o{ficial is an individual who is indirectly
involved in the governmental decision, the applicable materiality standard is found at
regulation I 8705.3(b)(3):
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $1,000 or more; or
(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under. . . [regulation] I 8705.2(b)."
Note that the analysis in regulation 18705.2(b) is to be applied to real property
owned or leased by an individual who is a source of income to a public official. Under
regulation 18705.2b), a decision is presumed not to have a material financial effect on
real property, when that property is indirectly involved in a govemmental decision. This
presumption may be rebutted by proof that specific circumstances exist regarding the
financial effect of a proposed decision and the nature of the real property which make it
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on that real
property.
Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered are found in
regulation 18705.2@)(2XA) - (C):
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels,
air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."

An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally''exception. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision upon a public official's economic interests is
indistinguishable from the effect of the decision on a significant segment of the public
generally. (Section 87103; regulation 18707(a).) Under this two-pronged exception, if a
"significant segment" of the jurisdiction or the official's election district is affected by
the govemmental decision in "substantially the same mannet''as it would affect the
public official, the public official mayparticipate in the decision.
Mayor Procter and Vice-Mayor Aguirre each have a conflict of interest based on
their interest in real property. Council members Cook, Krause and Luna do not have a
conflict of interest on that basis. However, it is possible that the presumption of no
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect with respect to their economic interest in
real iroperty may be rebutted by the Project's impact on traffic flow. Thus, our analysis
of the public generally exception will consider its application to economic interests
arising from real property, as applied to all of these public officials. In addition,
Councilmernber Cook's economic interests include the purchaser of his former residence,
who is a source of income to him. We will also include this economic interest in our
analysis of the public generally exception.
General RuIe, Part 1- Significant Segment
Real Property - All Oficials
Specific subdivisions of regulation 18707.1define what constitutes a "significant
segment," varying according to the economic interest involved. When the economic
interest which gives rise to the conflict of interest is an interest in real property, the
significant segment is defined as:
"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency."
(Subdivision 187 07 .t @XlXBXi) - (ii).)

adjacent to the Project's proposed boundaries. The Project overall will have significant

decisions will affect residents located proximate to both the traffic corridors and the

You state that there are 1,690 property owners located within 500 feet of the
city's seven traffic corridors that would be affected by this Project. You also indicate that
according to year 2000 U.S. census data, there are 6,142 property owners in the city.
Thus, 27.5% of the property owners within the city live within 500 feet of the seven

interests in real property.
Individuals Who Are Sources of Income -- Councilmember Cook
When the economic interest which gives rise to the conflict of interest is an
individual who is a source of income or a source of gifts to a public official, the

"(i) Ten percent or more of the population in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency or the district the o{ficial represents; or
(ii) 5,000 individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction."
(Subdivisions I 8707.1(bxl)(AXi) - (ii).)
As to Councilmember Cook's economic interest in the purchaser of his former
residence (a source of income to him), the appropriate "significant segment" of the public
generally is that defined immediately above. You indicate that according to year 2000

Given the city's average household size, this means 6,886 individuals reside within the

General Rule, Part2 - Substantially the Same Manner
After determining that a significant segment will be affected by a decision, it is
next necessary to evaluate if the public official's economic interest will be affected in
substantially the same manner as the rest of the segmant. If the answer is "yes" as to
each economic interest identified, then the effect of the decision is not distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally and the public official mayparticipate in the
decision. (Regulation 18707(b)(a).) To assess whether a significant segment will be
financially affected in substantially the same manner, all measurable effects from the
decision must be identified.
Real Property - All Officials
As noted above, Mayor Procter and Vice-Mayor Aguirre each have an interest in
real property located within 500 feet of the proposed boundaries of the Fagan Canyon
Project. Assuming that properties situated in the general area of the project will be
affected by these decisions in a manner that is substantially similar to the effect on the
officials' properties, you state that only 283 property owners are located within 500 feet
of the proposed boundaries of the project. This constitutes approximately 8 percent of
the homeowners in the jurisdiction. Please note, however, that comparing financial
effects is necessarily a factual process. If these officials determine that other

property owners or homeowners will be affected in substantially the s,rme manner.
However, absent a facfual assessment by these officials, it does not appear on its face that
the "public generally" exception applies to Mayor Procter and Vice-Mayor Aguirre.
In contrast to the analysis applied above, the concem with respect to the

property to the Project's proposed boundaries (since these officials are located more than

throughout the city. Thus, for council mernbers Cook, Krause and Luna, it is appropriate
to look at the impacts of these decisions on properties located in the general area of the

owners are located within 500 feet of these seven traffic corridors. Even if we were to

corridors strongly suggests that more than 10Yo of all property owners in the city will be
financially affected in substantially the same manner as these officials and the "public

With respect to Councilmember Cook's source of income (the purchaser of his

8. Are the officials legally required to participate in the decision?
The "legally required participation" exception permits an official who is
otherwise disqualified from making a goveflrmental decision to participate in the decision
when the official's participation is legally required. (Section 87101; regulation 18703.)
This exception (step eight of our standard conflict-of-interest analysis) is narrowly
construed and applies only when it is legally impossible for the decision to be made
without the participation of a disqualified official. Tlpically this exception is invoked
when, due to disqualification, an agency is unable to convene a quorum of its memb"rs.'u
In the present circumstances, three of the city council's five members
(Councilmembers Cook, Krause and Luna) may be able to participate. These three
constitute a quorum of the city council and for that reason, the "legally required
participation" exception may not be invoked by either Mayor Procter or Vice Mayor
Aguirre unless the decision at issue requires participation by a super-majority or a
unanimous vote. However, if the required number ofparticipating officials cannot be
achieved because of conflicts of interest, the "legally required participation" rule may be
invoked.lT
